title: Shapes with Math
primer: Every shape in a fragment shader is really just a mathematical condition: is this pixel inside or outside a region? The key tools are distance() to measure how far a point is from a reference, step() to make a hard binary decision, and smoothstep() to blend that edge into a silky anti-aliased transition. Together these three functions let you draw circles, rings, and rounded rectangles without a single triangle.

challenges.title: Draw a circle
challenges.description: Draw a white circle of radius 0.3 centered at (0.5, 0.5) on a black background. Use distance() to measure each pixel's distance from the center, then step() to decide inside vs outside.
hints: `step(edge, x)` returns 0.0 if x < edge, and 1.0 if x >= edge. You want the *inside* of the circle to be white, so think about how to invert the result.
Try `1.0 - step(0.3, d)`. When d is small (inside the circle), step returns 0.0, so 1.0 - 0.0 = 1.0 (white). When d is large, step returns 1.0, so 1.0 - 1.0 = 0.0 (black).
challenges.unlocks_theory: `step(edge, x)` is a Heaviside step function — it returns a hard 0 or 1 with no in-between. This is fast but produces a jagged, aliased edge at the boundary. For smooth results you'll replace it with `smoothstep`, which interpolates over a small transition band.

challenges.title: Smooth circle with smoothstep
challenges.description: Replace the hard step edge with smoothstep to get an anti-aliased circle. The circle should have radius 0.3 and a smooth falloff over a 0.01 transition band at its edge.
hints: `smoothstep(edge0, edge1, x)` returns 0.0 when x <= edge0 and 1.0 when x >= edge1. By swapping the edges (edge0 > edge1) you invert the ramp: 1.0 inside and 0.0 outside.
Use `smoothstep(0.31, 0.29, d)` — when d is small (inside), the result is 1.0; when d is large (outside), it is 0.0, with a smooth transition between 0.29 and 0.31.
challenges.unlocks_theory: `smoothstep` uses a cubic Hermite curve (3t² - 2t³) between its two edge values. The result has zero derivative at both endpoints, so colors transition without a visible discontinuity — this is how GPU-drawn circles look pixel-perfect at any scale. The width of the transition band (here 0.02) controls how blurry or sharp the edge appears.

challenges.title: Ring (annulus)
challenges.description: Draw a white ring with inner radius 0.22 and outer radius 0.32, centered at (0.5, 0.5). The ring is the region between two concentric circles — pixels that are inside the outer circle but outside the inner circle.
hints: Build the inner mask the same way as the outer circle mask but with a smaller radius: `smoothstep(0.23, 0.21, d)`.
The ring is the outer disk with the inner disk punched out: `ring = outer - inner`. Because both masks are in [0,1], the difference is also in [0,1].
challenges.unlocks_theory: Subtracting one mask from another is a fundamental CSG (constructive solid geometry) operation in shader space. The same idea extends to boolean union (max), intersection (min), and complement (1 - mask). These operations let you build complex shapes from simple primitives entirely in the pixel shader.

challenges.title: Rounded rectangle
challenges.description: Draw a white rounded rectangle centered at (0.5, 0.5) with half-extents (0.3, 0.2) and corner radius 0.08. A rounded rect SDF equals length(max(q, 0.0)) - r where q = abs(uv - center) - halfSize.
hints: The rounded-rect SDF works by computing how far the point is from the nearest corner arc. `q = abs(p) - b + r` folds the space into one quadrant; `max(q, 0.0)` clamps negative components (inside the rect body) to zero.
Complete the SDF body: `return length(max(q, 0.0)) - r;`. The `- r` shifts the surface inward by the corner radius, rounding the corners.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Signed Distance Functions (SDFs) return negative values inside a shape and positive values outside. This sign makes boolean operations trivial (union = min, intersection = max) and gives you the distance to the surface for free — enabling outlines, glows, and soft shadows all from a single SDF evaluation per pixel.
